original_hypothesis.title: Airdrop Double-Dipping via Merkle Root Updates
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The `MerkleClaimable` contract allows the owner to update the Merkle root via `setConfig`. The claim hash is generated using `keccak256(abi.encode("CLAIM_TAIKO_AIRDROP", data))`, where `data` includes the user's allocation amount. If the owner updates the root to increase a user's allocation, the new hash will not be marked as claimed, allowing the user to claim the new amount IN ADDITION to their previously claimed amount, draining the airdrop vault.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This exposes the protocol to massive over-issuance if standard airdrop management practices (publishing cumulative amounts in updated trees) are followed instead of publishing delta amounts.
deployment_assumptions: Owner updates Merkle root with cumulative allocation amounts instead of delta amounts
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: DeployERC20Airdrop.s.sol shows standard Merkle tree deployment, but operational updates are not constrained.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Claim hash includes amount, allowing re-claiming if amount changes in new Merkle root

preconditions: Owner updates Merkle root with a new allocation amount for the user

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Changing the amount changes the hash, bypassing the `isClaimed` check
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: User receives `old_amount + new_amount` instead of just `new_amount`

economic_questions: Does the protocol intend to update Merkle roots post-launch?
Are the off-chain Merkle tree generators outputting cumulative or delta amounts?
validation_requirements: Simulate a root update and verify a user can claim twice

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single block execution after root update

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Sufficient tokens in the airdrop vault
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after the owner updates the Merkle root

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Vault depletion preventing others from claiming
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All tokens in the airdrop vault

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Review off-chain Merkle tree generation scripts

validation_result.mechanism: per-hash claim tracking allows re-claiming on merkle root update
validation_result.impact_summary: When the owner updates the merkle root via setConfig with a different allocation for a user, the user can claim again because isClaimed is keyed by hash(user, amount), not by user address alone. This enables double-dipping of airdrop tokens.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The MerkleClaimable contract tracks claims using a hash that includes the claim data (user address + amount). The setConfig function allows the owner to update the merkle root at any time. When the merkle root is updated with a different allocation amount for the same user, the claim hash changes (because the amount is different), bypassing the isClaimed check. This allows the user to claim under both the old and new merkle roots.

The defect exists because the contract provides two features that are incompatible: (1) merkle root updates via setConfig, and (2) per-hash claim tracking instead of per-user claim tracking. A proper implementation for updatable merkle roots should either track claims per-user or disallow root updates entirely.

For ERC20Airdrop, tokens are transferred immediately on each claim, so the user receives old_amount + new_amount instead of just new_amount. For ERC20Airdrop2, claimedAmount[user] += amount accumulates across claims, giving the user more withdrawal allowance than intended.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal at MerkleClaimable.sol lines 63-64 is confirmed. The hash computation at line 77 includes the data parameter (which contains user and amount), and the isClaimed check at line 79 uses this hash. The setConfig function at lines 56-63 allows unrestricted merkle root updates by the owner. These two mechanisms together create the defect.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The claim hash includes the amount via the data parameter. When the merkle root is updated with a different amount for the same user, the hash changes, so isClaimed[newHash] is false, allowing the user to claim again.
Allows unrestricted merkle root updates with no mechanism to prevent double-claiming by users already in the old tree. No per-user claim tracking exists to prevent this.

The data passed to _verifyClaim is abi.encode(user, amount). Since amount is part of the hash, different amounts produce different hashes. Tokens are transferred directly, so each successful claim transfers the full amount.

claimedAmount[user] accumulates across multiple claims. If the user claims 100 under the old root and 200 under the new root, claimedAmount becomes 300, giving the user a larger withdrawal allowance than intended.

calling_sequence: 1. User claims amount=100 with proof against merkleRoot_v1
3. isClaimed[hash_v1] is false, merkle proof verifies, isClaimed[hash_v1] = true, 100 tokens transferred
4. Owner calls setConfig() to update merkleRoot to merkleRoot_v2 with user allocated 200 tokens
5. User claims amount=200 with proof against merkleRoot_v2
7. hash_v2 != hash_v1, so isClaimed[hash_v2] is false, merkle proof verifies against new root, 200 tokens transferred
8. User received 300 tokens total instead of intended 200
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability path goes through setConfig (MerkleClaimable.sol:56-63) updating the merkleRoot, then _verifyClaim (MerkleClaimable.sol:77-84) where the hash = keccak256(abi.encode('CLAIM_TAIKO_AIRDROP', data)) produces a different hash when data contains a different amount. Since isClaimed is keyed by this hash (line 79), a new amount bypasses the existing claim record. The token transfer at ERC20Airdrop.sol:68 or the claimedAmount accumulation at ERC20Airdrop2.sol:92 then gives the user additional tokens.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Owner updates Merkle root with cumulative allocation amounts instead of delta amounts
config_validation.deployment_evidence: DeployERC20Airdrop.s.sol initializes with bytes32(0) merkle root and zero timestamps (line: ERC20Airdrop.init, (address(0), 0, 0, bytes32(0), bridgedTko, vaultAddress)), indicating setConfig must be called separately. No deployment scripts constrain how subsequent merkle root updates are performed. The operational workflow for root updates is not defined in any deployment script.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is straightforward and valid: (1) User claims their allocation under merkle root v1. (2) Owner updates merkle root to v2 with a different allocation for the same user (e.g., cumulative amount). (3) User claims again under v2 because the hash is different. (4) User receives both amounts. This requires no special tools or capital - just standard claim transactions. The attack is feasible whenever the owner updates the merkle root with different amounts for users who have already claimed.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. Any merkle root update where existing users have different allocation amounts enables double-claiming. For ERC20Airdrop, tokens are transferred directly on each claim. For ERC20Airdrop2, the claimedAmount accumulates, giving users more withdrawal allowance. The total value at risk is the sum of all duplicated allocations across both the old and new merkle trees. In the worst case, the vault could be drained if the total double-claimed amount exceeds vault balance.
recommended_mitigations: Track claims per user address instead of per-hash: use mapping(address => bool) or mapping(address => uint256) to prevent any user from claiming more than once regardless of merkle root changes

Clear the isClaimed mapping when updating the merkle root (not practical due to mapping limitations), or use a nonce-based approach where each merkle root update increments a nonce included in the hash
Use a two-level claim tracking: isClaimed[merkleRoot][hash] to scope claims per merkle root version, combined with a per-user total cap
Document clearly that only delta amounts (not cumulative) should be used in updated merkle trees, if the current design is intentional
Consider removing the setConfig function entirely and deploying a new contract for each airdrop phase
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ERC20Airdrop with a token and vault. 2. Set merkle root v1 with Alice having 100 tokens. 3. Alice claims 100 tokens. 4. Owner calls setConfig with merkle root v2 where Alice has 200 tokens. 5. Alice claims 200 tokens. 6. Assert Alice received 300 tokens total (100 + 200) instead of 200. 7. Repeat for ERC20Airdrop2 and verify claimedAmount[Alice] == 300.
uncertainties: Whether the protocol team intends to ever update merkle roots post-launch is unknown from the codebase alone
Whether off-chain merkle tree generation uses cumulative or delta amounts is not determinable from on-chain code
The deployment script initializes with zero values, suggesting setConfig will be called later, but operational procedures are not codified
additional_testing_needed: Foundry test simulating merkle root update with cumulative amounts and verifying double-claim
Test with ERC20Airdrop2 to verify claimedAmount accumulation across merkle root updates
Test edge case where user has same amount in both old and new trees (hash would be the same, so claim would be blocked - only different amounts trigger the issue)
